data.content_ref: inline:{"claim":"The simultaneous collapse was a contingent conjuncture of heterogeneous local crises lacking unified causal structure, while Egyptian persistence reflects merely stochastic variation in institutional recovery rather than systemic immunity","mechanism":"no single mechanism operated across all cases; Mycenaean collapse involved Linear B administrative failure and warrior elite fragmentation, Hittite involved dynastic succession crises and Kaska pressure, Ugarit involved specific maritime vulnerability; Egypt experienced comparable stress (Sea Peoples invasions, labor strikes, tomb robberies) but recovered through Ramesses III's exceptional military expenditure that happened to succeed","scope":{"covers":["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia","Ugarit","Levantine city-states","Egypt"],"excludes":["unified theory of collapse","comparative civilizational pathology"]},"forbidden":[{"case":"identification of a genuinely common causal mechanism with archaeological or textual attestation across all collapsed polities that does not apply to Egypt","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"demonstration that Egyptian crisis indicators (workmen's strikes at Deir el-Medina, harem conspiracy, Libyan and Sea Peoples invasions) were substantially less severe than contemporaneous crises in Anatolia or Greece","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"evidence that post-Ramesses III Egypt experienced recovery to pre-crisis levels of territorial control, administrative coherence, or international prestige within two generations","eval":"rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes":"Radically anti-typological account denying 'collapse' as useful analytical category; treats Egyptian 'survival' as partial and temporary (Third Intermediate Period begins ~1077 BC); maximal skepticism toward structural explanation; faces criticism from A2 mechanist school and potentially from A1 for still implicitly comparing cases"}
